standards.title: Policy and Procedures for Access Control
standards.description: The organization SHALL develop, document, and disseminate an access control policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance. The policy SHALL be reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever significant changes occur. Supporting procedures SHALL facilitate the implementation of the access control policy and associated controls. This control establishes the governance foundation for all other access control measures and ensures organizational leadership commitment to access management.

standards.title: Account Management
standards.description: The organization SHALL define and document account types (individual, shared, group, system, guest, temporary, emergency), establish conditions for group and role membership, identify authorized users, and specify access authorizations. Account managers SHALL be designated for each information system. Accounts SHALL be created, enabled, modified, disabled, and removed in accordance with the organization's account management policy. The organization SHALL specifically authorize and monitor the use of guest and temporary accounts, terminating them when no longer required. Account management includes the identification of account types, assignment of account managers, establishing conditions for group membership, and monitoring of account usage.

standards.title: Account Management: Automated System Account Management
standards.description: The organization SHALL employ automated mechanisms to support the management of information system accounts. Automated account management includes the use of automated workflows for account provisioning and deprovisioning, automatic disabling of inactive accounts after a defined period, automatic notification of account creation and termination to designated personnel, and automated enforcement of account management policies. Automation reduces the risk of human error in account lifecycle management and ensures timely responses to personnel changes such as terminations, transfers, and role changes.

standards.title: Account Management: Automated Temporary and Emergency Account Removal
standards.description: The information system SHALL automatically remove or disable temporary and emergency accounts after a defined time period. Temporary accounts are established for a limited duration (e.g., contractor access for a project) and SHALL have explicit expiration dates set at creation. Emergency accounts are established in response to crisis situations and SHALL be automatically disabled when the crisis is resolved or after a maximum time limit. This prevents the accumulation of stale accounts that could be exploited by attackers who gain access to forgotten or abandoned credentials.

standards.title: Account Management: Disable Accounts After Inactivity
standards.description: The information system SHALL automatically disable accounts after a defined period of inactivity (typically 30-90 days depending on risk). Disabled accounts SHALL remain in a state that prevents login while preserving account data for audit and potential reactivation. Reactivation SHALL require verification of continued need and appropriate authorization. This control addresses the risk posed by dormant accounts which are prime targets for adversaries because their misuse is less likely to be noticed. Organizations SHALL define the inactivity threshold based on the sensitivity of the systems involved.

standards.title: Account Management: Automated Audit Actions
standards.description: The information system SHALL automatically audit account creation, modification, enabling, disabling, and removal actions and notify designated personnel or roles. Audit records SHALL capture the identity of the individual performing the action, the account affected, the type of change, and the timestamp. Notifications SHALL be sent to account managers and information system security officers. This automated auditing ensures accountability and provides the forensic evidence needed to investigate unauthorized account manipulation, which is a common early-stage attack technique.

standards.title: Account Management: Inactivity Logout
standards.description: The organization SHALL require that users log out after a defined period of expected inactivity or when sessions are no longer needed. This is distinct from AC-2(3) which addresses long-term account dormancy — AC-2(5) addresses per-session inactivity. The system SHALL automatically terminate a session or require reauthentication after the inactivity period expires. This control reduces the risk of session hijacking from unattended workstations and limits the window of opportunity for unauthorized access through abandoned sessions.

standards.title: Account Management: Privileged User Accounts
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish and administer privileged user accounts in accordance with a role-based access scheme. Privileged accounts SHALL be separated from regular user accounts, and users requiring privileged access SHALL use separate accounts for privileged and non-privileged activities. The organization SHALL monitor privileged role assignments, and privileged accounts SHALL be reviewed at a frequency commensurate with the risk (at minimum quarterly). Access to privileged functions SHALL be restricted to the minimum necessary for each role. This separation ensures that compromise of a regular user account does not immediately grant administrative access.

standards.title: Account Management: Restrictions on Shared and Group Accounts
standards.description: The organization SHALL restrict the use of shared and group accounts by establishing conditions that must be met before they are authorized. Shared accounts SHALL only be permitted when individual accountability can still be maintained through supplementary controls such as requiring individual authentication before accessing the shared account, logging all actions performed under the shared account with individual user attribution, and implementing time-limited check-out mechanisms. Group accounts for anonymous access SHALL be limited to systems where individual identification is not required and approved by the authorizing official.

standards.title: Account Management: Usage Conditions for Information System Accounts
standards.description: The organization SHALL enforce specified usage conditions for information system accounts, such as restricting account use to certain times of day, days of week, geographic locations, or specific devices. The information system SHALL enforce these conditions automatically and deny access when conditions are not met. For example, a financial system administrator account might only be usable during business hours from approved management workstations. These context-based restrictions implement a key zero trust principle: access decisions consider the full context of the request, not just the identity of the requester.

standards.title: Account Management: Account Monitoring for Atypical Usage
standards.description: The organization SHALL monitor information system accounts for atypical usage patterns and report anomalies to designated personnel. Atypical usage includes access outside normal working hours, access from unusual locations, access to resources not typically accessed by the account, unusually high volumes of data access or transfer, and privilege escalation patterns. This control implements user behavior analytics (UBA/UEBA) principles to detect compromised accounts or insider threats. The monitoring system SHALL establish baselines of normal behavior for each account and alert when significant deviations occur.

standards.title: Account Management: Disable Accounts for High-Risk Individuals
standards.description: The organization SHALL disable accounts of individuals determined to pose a significant risk within a defined time period (typically within 1 hour for high-risk determinations). High-risk individuals include users under investigation, users who have been terminated or reassigned, users whose credentials may have been compromised, and users who have violated acceptable use policies. The organization SHALL establish a process for receiving risk determinations from HR, legal, security, and management, and SHALL have automated or expedited manual processes for immediate account disabling. This is critical for preventing data exfiltration or sabotage by users who know their access is about to be revoked.

standards.title: Access Enforcement
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce approved authorizations for logical access to information and system resources in accordance with applicable access control policies. Access enforcement mechanisms include access control lists (ACLs), role-based access control (RBAC), attribute-based access control (ABAC), and mandatory access control (MAC). The enforcement mechanism SHALL deny access by default (deny-all, permit-by-exception) and SHALL be applied at every access decision point. This is the foundational access control mechanism that translates access control policies into technical enforcement, ensuring that users can only access resources they are explicitly authorized to use.

standards.title: Access Enforcement: Dual Authorization
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce dual authorization for defined critical operations, requiring approval from two or more authorized individuals before the operation can proceed. Dual authorization (also known as two-person integrity or four-eyes principle) is applied to operations that could have significant impact if performed maliciously or erroneously, such as modification of critical security configurations, deletion of large datasets, changes to access control policies, and key management operations. The system SHALL ensure that the two authorizing individuals have distinct roles and that neither can complete the operation alone.

standards.title: Access Enforcement: Discretionary Access Control
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce discretionary access control (DAC) allowing resource owners to control access to their own resources. Under DAC, the identity of the requestor and the access permissions defined by the resource owner determine whether access is granted. DAC policies are typically implemented through access control lists (ACLs) where resource owners can grant or revoke permissions to other subjects. While DAC provides flexibility, it has known limitations: users may inadvertently grant excessive permissions, and the propagation of permissions can be difficult to track. DAC is most appropriate for environments where data classification is not mandated by regulation.

standards.title: Access Enforcement: Role-Based Access Control
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce a role-based access control (RBAC) policy where access privileges are associated with defined roles rather than individual users. Users are assigned to roles based on their job functions, and roles are granted the minimum set of permissions required for that function. RBAC simplifies access management by decoupling permissions from individual identities, making it easier to manage access at scale and ensure consistency. Role definitions SHALL be reviewed periodically to ensure they reflect current organizational structure and job functions. RBAC is well-suited to organizations with well-defined job functions and relatively stable role definitions.

standards.title: Access Enforcement: Revocation of Access Authorizations
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce the revocation of access authorizations resulting from changes in security attributes of subjects and objects. When a user's role changes, their clearance is revoked, or a data classification changes, the system SHALL immediately update access decisions to reflect the new state. This is particularly critical in dynamic environments where attributes change frequently. The system SHALL not rely on cached access decisions that may be stale — access enforcement SHALL check current attributes at the time of each access decision, or within a bounded time window consistent with the organization's risk tolerance.

standards.title: Access Enforcement: Attribute-Based Access Control
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce an attribute-based access control (ABAC) policy where access decisions are based on attributes of the subject (user), object (resource), action (operation), and environment (context). Subject attributes may include role, clearance, department, and location; object attributes may include classification, sensitivity, and type; environment attributes may include time of day, network location, and threat level. ABAC provides fine-grained, dynamic access control that can adapt to changing conditions without requiring explicit permission assignments. ABAC is the access control model most aligned with zero trust principles because every access decision considers the full context.

standards.title: Information Flow Enforcement
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce approved authorizations for controlling the flow of information within the system and between connected systems based on information flow control policies. Information flow control regulates where information is allowed to travel, as distinct from access control which regulates who can access information. Flow control is enforced through mechanisms such as boundary protection devices, content filters, data loss prevention systems, and network segmentation. The system SHALL prevent unauthorized information flows including data exfiltration, cross-domain transfer of classified information, and flow of sensitive data to untrusted networks.

standards.title: Information Flow Enforcement: Object Security and Privacy Attributes
standards.description: The information system SHALL use security and privacy attributes associated with information, source, and destination objects as the basis for information flow control decisions. Attributes such as data classification labels, sensitivity tags, and privacy categories SHALL be bound to information objects and evaluated at every transfer point. The system SHALL ensure that attributes flow with the data — when information is copied, moved, or transformed, the security attributes SHALL be preserved or updated appropriately. This enables data-centric security where protections follow the data regardless of where it resides or how it is accessed.

standards.title: Separation of Duties
standards.description: The organization SHALL identify and document duties requiring separation and define information system access authorizations to support separation of duties. No single individual SHALL be able to complete a critical business process or security function alone when separation of duties is required. Common separations include: the person who requests access is not the person who approves it; the person who develops code is not the person who deploys it to production; the person who administers security controls is not the person who audits them. Separation of duties reduces the risk of fraud, error, and insider threats by requiring collusion between multiple parties to circumvent controls.

standards.title: Least Privilege
standards.description: The organization SHALL employ the principle of least privilege, allowing only authorized accesses for users and processes that are necessary to accomplish assigned organizational tasks. Each user, service account, and system process SHALL be granted only the minimum permissions needed to perform its intended function. Permissions SHALL be reviewed periodically and removed when no longer required (privilege creep prevention). The organization SHALL explicitly authorize access to security-relevant functions and security-relevant information. Least privilege is a foundational security principle that limits the blast radius of compromised accounts and reduces the attack surface available to both external and internal threats.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Authorize Access to Security Functions
standards.description: The organization SHALL explicitly authorize access to security-relevant functions (e.g., access control configuration, audit log management, security policy modification) and security-relevant information (e.g., security configuration data, audit records, vulnerability reports). Only designated security personnel SHALL have access to modify security configurations, and this access SHALL be separately tracked and audited. The authorization SHALL be documented, specifying which individuals or roles are authorized for each security function. This prevents unauthorized modification of security controls that could undermine the entire security posture of the system.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Non-Privileged Access for Non-Security Functions
standards.description: The organization SHALL require that users of information system accounts with access to security functions or security-relevant information use non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing non-security functions. System administrators SHALL use standard user accounts for everyday tasks such as email, web browsing, and document editing, and SHALL only use their administrative accounts when performing administrative tasks. This reduces the risk that privileged credentials are exposed to common attack vectors such as phishing, drive-by downloads, and malicious email attachments that target everyday user activities.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Network Access to Privileged Commands
standards.description: The organization SHALL authorize network access to privileged commands only for compelling operational needs and SHALL document the rationale. Remote administrative access SHALL be restricted to approved jump servers, bastion hosts, or privileged access workstations (PAWs). All network access to privileged functions SHALL be encrypted and authenticated at a level commensurate with the sensitivity of the operations being performed. The organization SHALL maintain a current list of network-accessible privileged commands and review it at a minimum quarterly. This control recognizes the elevated risk of remote administrative access and imposes additional restrictions beyond local privileged access.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Privileged Accounts
standards.description: The organization SHALL restrict privileged accounts on the information system to a defined set of personnel or roles. The number of privileged accounts SHALL be minimized, and the organization SHALL maintain a current registry of all privileged accounts with their justification and sponsoring authority. Privileged accounts include system administrators, database administrators, network administrators, security administrators, and any accounts with elevated access rights. Access to create or modify other accounts is itself a privileged function. The organization SHALL review the list of privileged accounts at least quarterly and remove or modify accounts that are no longer justified.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Review of User Privileges
standards.description: The organization SHALL review user privileges at a defined frequency (at minimum annually, quarterly for privileged accounts) to validate that the current set of privileges is still required and appropriate. The review SHALL verify that privileges are consistent with the user's current role and responsibilities and that no unauthorized privilege accumulation (privilege creep) has occurred. Identified excess privileges SHALL be removed promptly. The review process SHALL be documented, including the reviewer's identity, the date of review, the privileges reviewed, and the determination for each. Automated tooling for access certification campaigns is recommended to ensure comprehensive coverage.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Log Use of Privileged Functions
standards.description: The information system SHALL audit the execution of privileged functions, including all invocations of security-relevant commands and operations performed with elevated privileges. The audit records SHALL identify the user who executed the privileged function, the function executed, the parameters used, and the outcome. This logging is critical for detecting privilege abuse, investigating security incidents, and demonstrating compliance. The logs SHALL be protected from modification by privileged users (e.g., by forwarding to a separate, independently managed log store) to prevent administrators from covering their tracks.

standards.title: Least Privilege: Prohibit Non-Privileged Users from Executing Privileged Functions
standards.description: The information system SHALL prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions, including disabling, circumventing, or altering implemented security safeguards and countermeasures. This includes preventing non-privileged users from installing software, modifying system configurations, accessing security audit logs, and changing access control settings. The system SHALL enforce this prohibition through technical means (not solely procedural controls) and SHALL alert security personnel when attempted violations are detected. This control ensures that the technical enforcement of least privilege cannot be bypassed through social engineering or procedural workarounds.

standards.title: Unsuccessful Logon Attempts
standards.description: The information system SHALL enforce a limit of consecutive invalid logon attempts by a user during a defined time period and SHALL automatically lock the account or delay next logon attempt when the maximum number of unsuccessful attempts is exceeded. The organization SHALL define the maximum number of consecutive invalid attempts (typically 3-5 for privileged accounts, 5-10 for regular accounts) and the time period during which the counting occurs. When an account is locked, the organization SHALL require the user to unlock through an out-of-band mechanism or wait for an automatic unlock after a defined delay period. These measures protect against brute-force and credential-stuffing attacks.

standards.title: Unsuccessful Logon Attempts: Purge or Wipe Mobile Device
standards.description: The information system SHALL purge or wipe information from a mobile device after a defined number of consecutive unsuccessful device authentication attempts. This provides a last-resort data protection mechanism for lost or stolen mobile devices. The threshold for wiping is typically higher than for account lockout (e.g., 10 failed attempts) to reduce the risk of accidental data loss. Before wiping, the device SHOULD back up data to an enterprise backup service if connectivity is available. The organization SHALL inform users of this policy and ensure that enterprise data on mobile devices is independently recoverable through enterprise backup mechanisms.

standards.title: System Use Notification
standards.description: The information system SHALL display an approved system use notification message before granting access that provides privacy and security notices consistent with applicable laws, policies, and standards. The notification SHALL state that the user is accessing a monitored system, that unauthorized use is prohibited and subject to criminal and civil penalties, that use of the system constitutes consent to monitoring, and that information collected may be used for law enforcement purposes. The user SHALL be required to acknowledge the notification before proceeding. System use notifications establish legal standing for monitoring activities and set clear expectations for users regarding acceptable use.

standards.title: Concurrent Session Control
standards.description: The information system SHALL limit the number of concurrent sessions for each account to a defined number. The limit SHALL be based on the type of account and the risk associated with the system. For privileged accounts, concurrent sessions SHOULD be limited to one to prevent credential sharing and ensure accountability. When the maximum number of sessions is reached, the system SHALL deny additional session requests or terminate the oldest session. Concurrent session limits help detect credential theft (an attacker using stolen credentials while the legitimate user is also logged in) and prevent unauthorized credential sharing among multiple individuals.

standards.title: Device Lock
standards.description: The information system SHALL prevent further access to the system by initiating a session lock after a defined period of inactivity, and the session lock SHALL remain in effect until the user re-establishes access using established identification and authentication procedures. The session lock SHALL conceal the previously visible information with a publicly viewable image or blank screen. The inactivity threshold SHALL be configurable by the organization (typically 5-15 minutes for standard systems, shorter for high-security systems). Users SHALL also be able to manually initiate a device lock at any time. Device lock is a critical control for preventing unauthorized access to systems left unattended.

standards.title: Session Termination
standards.description: The information system SHALL automatically terminate a user session after defined conditions are met. Conditions for session termination include: exceeding the maximum session duration, reaching the inactivity timeout, receiving a termination request from the user, receiving an administrative termination command, detecting a security violation, or determining that the user's authentication credentials have been revoked. Upon session termination, the system SHALL release all system resources associated with the session. The system SHALL notify the user of the impending session termination when possible to prevent data loss from unsaved work.

standards.title: Permitted Actions Without Identification or Authentication
standards.description: The organization SHALL identify specific user actions that can be performed on the information system without identification or authentication consistent with organizational missions and business functions. These typically include viewing public-facing content, accessing system status pages, and submitting initial registration requests. All actions not explicitly identified as permissible without authentication SHALL require authentication. The list of permitted unauthenticated actions SHALL be documented, approved by the authorizing official, and reviewed at least annually. Minimizing unauthenticated access reduces the attack surface available to anonymous adversaries.

standards.title: Security and Privacy Attributes
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish and maintain security and privacy attributes for information stored in, processed by, or transmitted by the information system. These attributes (e.g., classification labels, sensitivity tags, handling caveats, privacy categories) provide the metadata needed for attribute-based access control (ABAC) decisions and information flow enforcement. The information system SHALL support the association of attributes with information in storage and in transit. Attributes SHALL be reviewable by authorized individuals and SHALL be maintainable by authorized individuals or automated processes. The integrity of security attributes SHALL be protected through integrity verification mechanisms.

standards.title: Remote Access
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish and document usage restrictions, configuration and connection requirements, and implementation guidance for each type of remote access allowed. The organization SHALL authorize each type of remote access before allowing connections. Remote access methods include VPN, remote desktop, SSH, and web-based access portals. All remote access sessions SHALL be encrypted using approved cryptographic mechanisms. The organization SHALL enforce remote access requirements through both technical controls and policy mechanisms. Remote access introduces additional risk because it extends the network perimeter and often traverses untrusted networks.

standards.title: Remote Access: Monitoring and Control
standards.description: The information system SHALL monitor and control remote access methods, including tracking which remote access connections are active, the users associated with each connection, the duration of each session, and the resources accessed. Automated monitoring SHALL alert security personnel to anomalous remote access patterns such as connections from unexpected geographic locations, connections outside normal business hours, or unusual data transfer volumes. The organization SHALL implement real-time or near-real-time monitoring of remote sessions and SHALL have the capability to terminate individual remote access sessions when policy violations or security incidents are detected.

standards.title: Remote Access: Protection of Confidentiality and Integrity Using Encryption
standards.description: The information system SHALL implement cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality and integrity of remote access sessions. All remote access connections SHALL use TLS 1.2 or higher, IPsec, or other approved encryption protocols. The encryption SHALL protect both the authentication credentials transmitted during session establishment and the data transmitted during the session. Certificate-based authentication SHALL be used where possible to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks. The organization SHALL ensure that remote access encryption configurations are consistent with FIPS 140-2/140-3 requirements for federal systems or equivalent standards for other organizations.

standards.title: Remote Access: Privileged Commands and Access
standards.description: The organization SHALL authorize the execution of privileged commands via remote access only for compelling operational needs and SHALL document the rationale in the security plan. Remote execution of privileged commands SHALL be restricted to designated jump servers or privileged access workstations. All remote privileged sessions SHALL be recorded (screen, keystroke, or command log) and SHALL be reviewed by security personnel. Multi-factor authentication SHALL be required for all remote privileged access, and session duration SHALL be limited. This control recognizes that remote privileged access represents one of the highest-risk activities in any IT environment.

standards.title: Remote Access: Disconnect or Disable Access
standards.description: The organization SHALL provide the capability to disconnect or disable remote access within a defined time period (typically within 15 minutes to 1 hour). This emergency disconnect capability is essential for incident response scenarios where remote access channels may be compromised or actively exploited. The organization SHALL define clear procedures and authorities for invoking the remote access disconnect, including who is authorized to make the decision, the process for communicating the disconnect, and procedures for restoring access after the incident is resolved. Automated disconnect triggers based on security event correlation SHOULD be implemented where feasible.

standards.title: Wireless Access
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish usage restrictions, configuration and connection requirements, and implementation guidance for wireless access. The organization SHALL authorize each type of wireless access before allowing connections. Wireless access SHALL be protected using authentication of users and devices before granting network access, encryption of wireless communications (WPA3 Enterprise or equivalent), and monitoring for rogue access points. The organization SHALL disable wireless networking interfaces when not needed and SHALL scan for unauthorized wireless connections at regular intervals. Wireless networks inherently extend the physical attack surface beyond the organization's controlled space.

standards.title: Access Control for Mobile Devices
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish usage restrictions, configuration requirements, connection requirements, and implementation guidance for mobile devices. Mobile device access control SHALL include device enrollment and registration, enforcement of device configuration policies (encryption, screen lock, OS version), containerization or separation of organizational and personal data, remote wipe capability for lost or stolen devices, and restrictions on which applications can access organizational data. Mobile device management (MDM) or enterprise mobility management (EMM) solutions SHALL be deployed to enforce these policies. The organization SHALL maintain an inventory of authorized mobile devices and their current compliance status.

standards.title: Use of External Systems
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish terms and conditions for authorized individuals to access the information system from external systems (e.g., partner networks, personal devices, public computers). The organization SHALL verify the implementation of required security controls on external systems before allowing connections where feasible, or accept the risk and implement compensating controls where verification is not feasible. Limitations SHALL include restricting the types of data that can be accessed from external systems, requiring VPN connections, prohibiting download of sensitive data to external systems, and implementing data loss prevention controls. Cloud services used by the organization are also considered external systems under this control.

standards.title: Information Sharing
standards.description: The organization SHALL facilitate information sharing by enabling authorized users to determine whether access authorizations assigned to a sharing partner match the information's access restrictions. When automated mechanisms are not available, the organization SHALL employ manual processes to assist users in making sharing decisions. The information system SHALL enforce information-sharing decisions by the user or organization based on access authorization of the sharing partner. Information sharing controls are particularly important in multi-tenant and cross-organizational environments where data must flow between parties with different security domains and trust levels.

standards.title: Publicly Accessible Content
standards.description: The organization SHALL designate individuals authorized to post information onto a publicly accessible system, train authorized individuals to ensure that publicly accessible information does not contain nonpublic information, review the proposed content of information prior to posting to ensure that nonpublic information is not included, review publicly accessible information at a defined frequency (at least quarterly) to ensure no nonpublic information is present, and remove nonpublic information promptly upon discovery. This control prevents inadvertent disclosure of sensitive information through public-facing systems, which is a common source of data breaches and compliance violations.

standards.title: Access Control Decisions
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish procedures for access control decisions and SHALL implement a defined access decision process for all access requests. Access control decisions SHALL be based on the identity of the requestor, the resource being accessed, the action being requested, and the context of the request (time, location, device posture). The organization SHALL determine whether access control decisions are made locally at the point of access, centrally through a policy decision point (PDP), or through a combination. Centralized policy decision points are preferred in zero trust architectures because they ensure consistent policy enforcement and provide a single point for policy updates and audit.

standards.title: Reference Monitor
standards.description: The information system SHALL implement a reference monitor that mediates all access to objects by subjects, is tamperproof, is always invoked (cannot be bypassed), and is small enough to be subject to analysis and testing. The reference monitor is the conceptual mechanism that enforces the access control policy — every access request passes through it, and it cannot be circumvented. In practice, the reference monitor may be implemented through a combination of operating system kernel access control modules, database access control mechanisms, application-level authorization middleware, and API gateways. The reference monitor principle ensures that access control enforcement is complete and cannot be bypassed by accessing resources through alternative paths.

standards.title: Identification and Authentication Policy and Procedures
standards.description: The organization SHALL develop, document, and disseminate an identification and authentication policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, and compliance. The policy SHALL define the acceptable authenticator types for each system based on risk assessment, the identity proofing requirements for different user populations, and the authentication assurance levels required for access to systems of varying sensitivity. Supporting procedures SHALL specify the operational processes for credential issuance, renewal, revocation, and recovery. The policy SHALL be reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever significant changes to the authentication landscape occur.

standards.title: Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)
standards.description: The information system SHALL uniquely identify and authenticate organizational users or processes acting on behalf of organizational users. Each user SHALL be assigned a unique identifier that is not shared with other users and that persists for the life of the user account. Authentication SHALL verify that the user is who they claim to be before granting access. The authentication mechanism SHALL be appropriate to the sensitivity of the system — ranging from password-based authentication for low-sensitivity systems to multi-factor cryptographic authentication for high-sensitivity systems. Identification and authentication SHALL occur before any other access control decisions are made.

standards.title: Identification and Authentication: Multi-Factor Authentication to Privileged Accounts
standards.description: The information system SHALL implement multi-factor authentication for network access to privileged accounts. Multi-factor authentication requires two or more different factors from: something you know (password, PIN), something you have (hardware token, smart card, phone), or something you are (biometric). For privileged accounts, MFA SHALL be required for all access regardless of the access method (local console, remote desktop, API, SSH). Phishing-resistant methods (FIDO2, certificate-based) are strongly preferred for privileged accounts. The MFA mechanism SHALL be resistant to replay attacks and SHALL not rely solely on SMS-based out-of-band delivery for privileged account authentication.

standards.title: Identification and Authentication: Access to Accounts — Separate Device
standards.description: The information system SHALL implement multi-factor authentication such that one of the factors is provided by a device separate from the system gaining access. This means a password entered on a laptop combined with a push notification on a phone, or a password combined with a hardware security key physically separate from the workstation. The separate device requirement prevents single-point-of-compromise attacks where malware on the accessing device could intercept all authentication factors. For highest assurance, the separate device SHOULD be a hardware cryptographic authenticator (FIDO2 key) that provides verifier impersonation resistance, preventing even sophisticated phishing attacks from succeeding.

standards.title: Authenticator Management
standards.description: The organization SHALL manage information system authenticators by verifying the identity of the individual, group, role, service, or device receiving the authenticator as part of the initial distribution; establishing initial authenticator content (e.g., initial password); ensuring that authenticators have sufficient strength for their intended use; establishing administrative procedures for lost, compromised, or damaged authenticators; changing default authenticators prior to first use; and changing or refreshing authenticators at defined intervals or when compromise is suspected. The organization SHALL protect authenticator content from unauthorized disclosure and modification. All authenticator lifecycle events SHALL be logged for audit purposes.

standards.title: Authenticator Management: Password-Based Authentication
standards.description: For password-based authentication, the information system SHALL enforce minimum password complexity (length of at least 8 characters for user-chosen, with no maximum less than 64 characters), store passwords using an approved one-way key derivation function that is salted and computationally expensive, prohibit password reuse for a minimum of 24 generations, and check passwords against lists of commonly used, expected, or compromised values. The system SHALL NOT require periodic password changes unless there is evidence of compromise, as forced rotation tends to lead to weaker passwords. The system SHALL allow pasting of passwords to facilitate use of password managers.

standards.title: Identification and Authentication (Non-Organizational Users)
standards.description: The information system SHALL uniquely identify and authenticate non-organizational users or processes acting on behalf of non-organizational users. Non-organizational users include contractors, partners, customers, and users from federated identity providers. The authentication requirements for non-organizational users SHALL be commensurate with the risk associated with the access being granted. Federation SHALL be preferred over provisioning local accounts for non-organizational users when a trust relationship exists. When local accounts are necessary, they SHALL be subject to the same lifecycle management controls as organizational accounts, including regular review, inactivity disabling, and prompt deprovisioning at the end of the business relationship.

standards.title: Re-authentication
standards.description: The information system SHALL require re-authentication when defined conditions are met, including but not limited to: a defined time period has elapsed since the last authentication, the user requests access to a particularly sensitive resource, a significant change in the user's context is detected (new device, new location, unusual behavior), or the system's risk assessment indicates elevated risk. Re-authentication SHALL require the user to present their current authenticator(s) at the same or higher assurance level as the original authentication. The system SHALL NOT rely on the continued validity of a session token alone to infer continued user presence — re-authentication confirms that the original authenticated user is still in control of the session.

standards.title: Identity Proofing
standards.description: The organization SHALL identity-proof users requiring accounts on organizational information systems. Identity proofing verifies that the individual is who they claim to be before credentials are issued. The level of identity proofing SHALL be commensurate with the sensitivity of the systems the account will access. For general user accounts, self-assertion with email verification may suffice (equivalent to IAL1). For accounts accessing sensitive data or privileged functions, government-issued photo ID verification is required (equivalent to IAL2). For accounts with access to critical infrastructure, in-person verification with SUPERIOR evidence is required (equivalent to IAL3). Identity proofing results SHALL be documented and the evidence retained according to the organization's records retention policy.

standards.title: Identity Proofing: Identity Evidence Validation and Verification
standards.description: The organization SHALL validate and verify identity evidence presented during the identity proofing process. Validation confirms that the evidence is genuine and has not been altered — this includes checking security features, verifying the document against the issuing source's database, and confirming that the document has not been reported as lost or stolen. Verification confirms that the evidence belongs to the applicant — this includes comparing the applicant's physical appearance to the photograph on the evidence, performing biometric comparison, or verifying knowledge that only the legitimate holder of the evidence would possess. The level of validation and verification SHALL match the target identity assurance level.

standards.title: Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
standards.description: The organization SHALL establish and manage cryptographic keys used for authentication, session protection, and credential storage. Key management SHALL address the entire key lifecycle: generation using approved random number generators, distribution through secure channels, storage in hardware security modules or secure enclaves where possible, usage logging and monitoring, rotation at defined intervals or upon suspected compromise, and destruction through approved sanitization methods. For identity infrastructure specifically, this includes PKI certificate management for TLS, code signing, and client authentication; symmetric keys for TOTP/HOTP authenticators; signing keys for SAML assertions and JWTs; and encryption keys for credential databases. Key management failures in identity systems can compromise the entire authentication infrastructure.

standards.title: Session Authenticity
standards.description: The information system SHALL protect the authenticity of communications sessions. Session authenticity ensures that data exchanged during a session comes from the expected parties and has not been modified in transit. For identity, this requires that session tokens be cryptographically bound to the authenticated user and protected against theft and replay. TLS SHALL be used for all session traffic. Session identifiers SHALL be sufficiently random (at least 128 bits of entropy) to prevent prediction. The system SHALL detect and reject session tokens that have been tampered with. At higher assurance levels, session tokens SHALL be bound to the TLS connection through token binding or channel binding to prevent session hijacking even when tokens are exfiltrated.

standards.title: Session Authenticity: Unique Session Identifiers with Randomization
standards.description: The information system SHALL generate a unique session identifier for each session with a random value using an approved random number generator. Session identifiers SHALL have at least 128 bits of entropy to make prediction computationally infeasible. The session identifier SHALL be generated after successful authentication — pre-authentication session identifiers SHALL be invalidated upon login (to prevent session fixation attacks). The session identifier SHALL not contain sensitive information or be derivable from user attributes. Session identifiers SHALL be transmitted only over encrypted channels and SHALL not appear in URLs, log files, or error messages. Each new authentication event SHALL generate a new session identifier, even if a valid session already exists.
